does: This paper introduces a three-source interaction framework to systematically evaluate how large language models balance internal parametric knowledge, user assertions, and document information simultaneously. Rather than studying these sources in isolation, the authors conduct empirical experiments on 27 LLMs across 3 families using 2 datasets to characterize how models navigate conflicts among all three sources. The key findings establish that most models privilege document assertions over user assertions, with this preference strengthened by post-training. The behavioral analysis reveals that models struggle to discriminate between helpful and harmful external information, showing general impressionability. The authors propose fine-tuning on diverse sources as a mitigation strategy to improve models' ability to balance these competing knowledge inputs reliably.
connects: This work builds on prior research examining knowledge conflicts in LLMs, extending beyond earlier binary paradigms that studied only pairwise conflicts between parametric knowledge and single external sources. It responds to limitations in sycophancy studies, which typically explored user influence in isolation, and knowledge conflict research that examined document-knowledge interactions separately. The paper connects to retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) literature and chat-based system design, where handling multiple information sources is practically critical. It also engages with post-training methodology research showing how instruction-tuning and alignment procedures shape model behavior. The safety-focused perspective links to work on robustness and adversarial inputs, building on the understanding that external information can be misused or contradictory.
enables: This framework opens research into more realistic multi-source knowledge dynamics in LLMs, moving beyond simplified binary conflict scenarios toward actual deployment settings. The findings motivate development of better training and fine-tuning approaches that teach models principled discrimination between information sources based on reliability and relevance rather than source type. The work enables future investigation of how to align models with appropriate source hierarchies for different contexts. It points toward improved safety mechanisms for RAG systems and user-interactive applications by identifying failure modes in current architectures. The behavioral analysis establishing impressionability suggests future work on robustness techniques and prompt engineering strategies. The paper also enables comparative analysis across model families and scales, informing architectural choices for systems requiring nuanced knowledge integration.
